annotations.label: Lagrange's theorem

anchor.value: Lagrange's theorem — If H is a subgroup

annotations.note: Mathlib states this verbatim as Lagrange's theorem: `Nat.card s ∣ Nat.card α` for any subgroup `s : Subgroup α`.

annotations.label: Lagrange's theorem (statement in lead)

anchor.snippet: Lagrange's theorem states that if H is a subgroup of any finite group G

annotations.note: Same statement as the boxed theorem; the docstring in Coset/Card.lean explicitly labels it `**Lagrange's Theorem**`.

annotations.label: Index variant of Lagrange's theorem

anchor.snippet: not only is

annotations.note: `H.index * Nat.card H = Nat.card G` directly formalizes the equality |G:H|·|H| = |G|; the companion `card_eq_card_quotient_mul_card_subgroup` states it via `α ⧸ s`.

annotations.label: Infinite case via cardinal numbers

anchor.snippet: This variant holds even if

annotations.note: The noncomputable equivalence `α ≃ (α ⧸ s) × s` gives the cardinal-arithmetic Lagrange formula in disguise, but there is no Mathlib lemma asserting `Cardinal.mk α = Cardinal.mk (α ⧸ s) * Cardinal.mk s` directly.

annotations.label: Left cosets partition G

anchor.snippet: the set of left cosets forms a partition of

annotations.note: The setoid `QuotientGroup.leftRel s` (with `α ⧸ s := Quotient (leftRel s)`) realises the left-coset partition as equivalence classes by construction.

annotations.label: Extension of Lagrange's theorem

anchor.value: Extension of Lagrange's theorem — If H i

annotations.note: For `H ≤ K`, `H.relIndex K * K.index = H.index` formalises the tower equality [G:H] = [G:K]·[K:H].

annotations.label: Decomposition of cosets between three subgroups

anchor.snippet: each left coset of H decomposes into

annotations.note: Mathlib has the numerical relation `relIndex_mul_index` but not an explicit lemma asserting that each left K-coset decomposes into [K:H] left H-cosets as a set partition.

annotations.label: Recovering original Lagrange equation

anchor.snippet: we can recover the original equation

annotations.note: Setting `H = ⊥` in `relIndex_mul_index` together with `index_mul_card` recovers the standard Lagrange equation.

annotations.label: Order of an element divides group order

anchor.snippet: the order of any element

annotations.note: `orderOf x ∣ Nat.card G` directly formalises the divisibility of element order by group order.

annotations.label: Fermat's little theorem and Euler's theorem as consequences

anchor.snippet: This can be used to prove Fermat's little theorem

annotations.note: Euler's theorem `x ^ φ n ≡ 1 [MOD n]` (with `ZMod.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one` as Fermat's little) is stated in Mathlib and labelled the Fermat-Euler totient theorem.

annotations.label: Groups of prime order are cyclic and simple

anchor.snippet: any group of prime order is cyclic and simple

annotations.note: `isCyclic_of_prime_card` gives the cyclic conclusion and `isSimpleGroup_of_prime_card` (in Mathlib.GroupTheory.SpecificGroups.Cyclic) gives simplicity.

annotations.label: Infinitude of primes via Lagrange

anchor.snippet: there are infinitely many primes

annotations.note: The infinitude of primes itself is formalised, but the specific Lagrange-based argument (via |(Z/pZ)*| dividing) is not given as a distinct Mathlib proof.

annotations.label: Failure of the converse of Lagrange's theorem

anchor.section: Existence of subgroups of given order
anchor.snippet: there does not necessarily exist a subgroup of

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma directly asserts the existence of a divisor d of |G| with no subgroup of order d (the failure of the converse is folklore around the A4 counterexample).

annotations.label: CLT group

anchor.section: Existence of subgroups of given order
anchor.snippet: "Converse of Lagrange's Theorem" (CLT) group

annotations.note: Grep for `CLT` in Mathlib/GroupTheory returns no matches; no definition of CLT group exists.

annotations.label: CLT groups are solvable; supersolvable implies CLT

anchor.section: Existence of subgroups of given order
anchor.snippet: a CLT group must be solvable

annotations.note: Neither `CLT` nor `supersolvable` appears in Mathlib/GroupTheory; this implication chain is absent.

annotations.label: Cauchy's theorem (partial converse)

anchor.section: Existence of subgroups of given order
anchor.snippet: Cauchy's theorem guarantees the existence of an element

annotations.note: The root-namespace lemma is explicitly documented as Cauchy's theorem and provides an element of order `p` whenever `p ∣ |G|`.

annotations.label: Sylow's theorem (partial converse)

anchor.section: Existence of subgroups of given order
anchor.snippet: Sylow's theorem extends this to the existence of a subgroup

annotations.note: Mathlib provides the existence of subgroups of every prime-power order dividing |G| as a generalisation of Sylow's first theorem.

annotations.label: Hall's theorems (partial converse)

anchor.section: Existence of subgroups of given order
anchor.snippet: Hall's theorems assert the existence of a subgroup

annotations.note: Mathlib has Schur-Zassenhaus but no theorem asserting existence of Hall π-subgroups in solvable groups; grep for `hallSubgroup`/`exists_hall` returns nothing.

annotations.label: A4 has no subgroup of order 6

anchor.section: Counterexample of the converse of Lagrange's theorem
anchor.snippet: We will examine the alternating group

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma directly states that `alternatingGroup (Fin 4)` has no subgroup of order 6, though `Nat.card ≠ 6` appears as a hypothesis in the alternating-simplicity development.

annotations.label: Klein four-group V in A4

anchor.section: Counterexample of the converse of Lagrange's theorem
anchor.snippet: the non-cyclic subgroup of

annotations.note: The Klein four subgroup of `alternatingGroup α` is defined and shown to satisfy `IsKleinFour`.

annotations.label: Lagrange's polynomial result

anchor.snippet: the number of different polynomials that are obtained is always a factor of

annotations.note: Lagrange's original 1770/71 polynomial-orbit-count result is not formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss's special case for (Z/pZ)*

anchor.snippet: Carl Friedrich Gauss proved Lagrange's theorem for the special case

annotations.note: Gauss's special case (Fermat's little theorem in (Z/pZ)*) is Mathlib's `ZMod.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one`.

annotations.label: Cauchy's proof for symmetric group

anchor.snippet: Augustin-Louis Cauchy proved Lagrange's theorem for the symmetric group

annotations.note: Mathlib has Lagrange's theorem in full generality but not Cauchy's historical 1844 specialisation to the symmetric group.

annotations.label: Jordan's proof for permutation groups

anchor.snippet: Camille Jordan finally proved Lagrange's theorem for the case of any permutation group

annotations.note: Jordan's historical proof for permutation groups is subsumed by Mathlib's general Lagrange theorem and is not formalised as a distinct result.
